title: A resolution establishing the Senate Human Rights Commission.
plain_summary: This bill creates a new group called the Senate Human Rights Commission that will be made up of ten senators. The commission's job is to talk about human rights problems around the world and help people learn about when human rights are being violated. It will have two leaders, one from each political party, to make sure both sides work together. The commission can spend up to two hundred thousand dollars each year and will continue working until 2029.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Rules and Administration. (text: CR S1632-1633)

sections.title: Section 1 Senate Human Rights Commission
sections.text:
This section creates a new group in the Senate called the Senate Human Rights Commission. The Commission will be made up of ten Senators and will focus on human rights issues around the world.

The Commission's main jobs will be to provide a place where Senators from both political parties can discuss international human rights problems. It will also watch what is happening with human rights in different countries and tell people about serious human rights violations by holding meetings, hearings, and private discussions. The Commission must keep records of all its meetings and activities. It will work with other congressional committees, the executive branch, human rights organizations, civil society groups, non-governmental organizations, and think tanks to promote human rights efforts in the Senate.

The Commission cannot create or vote on laws. It cannot take legislative action on any bill or resolution. It also cannot interfere with the work of any existing Senate committees.

The Commission will have ten Senate members. The President of the Senate will choose these members and can also remove them. After talking with the majority leader, the President of the Senate will pick five members from the majority party. One of these five will be chosen as a co-chairperson. After talking with the minority leader, the President of the Senate will pick five members from the minority party. One of these five will also be chosen as a co-chairperson.

Each member's term will end on the last day of the Congress session when they were appointed, unless they stop being a Senator, leave the Commission, or get removed. If a position becomes empty, it will be filled the same way the original appointment was made. If a Commission member wants to quit, they must send a written notice to the President of the Senate.

The Commission will have two co-chairpersons. The majority leader of the Senate will appoint one co-chairperson and can remove that person. The minority leader of the Senate will appoint the other co-chairperson and can also remove that person. Each co-chairperson's term will end on the last day of the Congress session when they were appointed, unless they stop being a Senator, leave the Commission, quit as co-chairperson, or get removed. These appointments will be printed in the Congressional Record. If a co-chairperson position becomes empty, it will be filled the same way the original appointment was made.

The Commission can hire staff and spend money as needed to do its work. Staff pay and spending limits will follow the same rules that apply to Senate committee employees. The Commission can pay for receptions, meals, and food expenses only when these are actual expenses needed for official Commission business. Money received to pay back these expenses will not count as income, and the expenses cannot be used as tax deductions.

Each co-chairperson can choose one professional staff member. If this staff member already works for a Senator or Senate committee, they will keep getting paid by their current employer, but that employer will get paid back from Commission funds. Each professional staff member must serve all Commission members and do other tasks assigned by the co-chairperson who chose them.

Commission expenses will be paid from the Senate's Contingent Fund using the Miscellaneous Items account. Both co-chairpersons must approve payment vouchers, except for employee salaries that are paid yearly. The Commission cannot spend more than two hundred thousand dollars in any fiscal year for employees and expenses.

This section will stop working on January first, two thousand twenty-nine.
